---
title: "GPP_Analysis"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)
```

```{R}
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)
library(purrr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(Rssa)
library(ggpmisc)
library(tibble)
```

## Objective
The object of the analysis is to determine the Start of Season (SOS) with seasonal GPP cycle oriented at the analysis of Gu et al. (2022). GPP measurements will be smoothed first, then the SOS will be categorized as 15% of daily maximum GPP. In a next step the late spring frost events will be identified and then finally the GPP in the following 30-180 days will be analyzed and compared with the long term average in the respective time span. To remain consistent with Gu et al. (2022), daily GPP time series were smoothed using Singular Spectrum Analysis (SSA) prior to extracting the start of season (SOS) from the smoothed seasonal trajectory. As a sensitivity analysis, an alternative integral smoothing approach based on the smoothed cumulative GPP curve was additionally evaluated.

### Read in CSV-Files of selected stations
```{R}
files <- list.files(
  "../data/test_data",
  pattern = "^FLX_.*\\.csv$",
  full.names = TRUE
)

df_all_selected_stations <- lapply(files, function(f) {
  read_csv(f) |>
    mutate(
      source_file = basename(f),
      site_id = str_extract(basename(f), "(?<=FLX_)[A-Z]{2}-[A-Za-z0-9]+(?=_FLUXDATAKIT)")
    )
}) |>
  bind_rows()

df_all_selected_stations <- df_all_selected_stations |>
  mutate(date = ymd(TIMESTAMP))|>
  filter(!(month(date) == 2 & day(date) == 29))|>
  mutate(
    year = year(date),
    doy = yday(date),
    doy = if_else(leap_year(date) & month(date) >= 3, doy - 1L, doy)
  )
```

## Check data quality of GPP recordings
```{R}
# quality control of yearly data
df_qc_year <- df_all_selected_stations |>
  group_by(site_id, year) |>
  summarise(
    n_days_total = n(),
    n_gpp_dt = sum(!is.na(GPP_DT_VUT_REF)),
    n_gpp_nt = sum(!is.na(GPP_NT_VUT_REF)),
    frac_gpp_dt = n_gpp_dt / n_days_total,
    frac_gpp_nt = n_gpp_nt / n_days_total,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# mark all years, where <300 days GPP measurements
df_qc_year <- df_qc_year |>
  mutate(
    pass_annual_dt = n_gpp_dt >= 300,
    pass_annual_nt = n_gpp_nt >= 300
  )

## quality control of spring data (DOY 60-150)
df_qc_spring <- df_all_selected_stations |>
  filter(doy >= 60, doy <=150) |>
  group_by(site_id, year) |> 
  summarise(
    n_spring_days = n(),
    n_spring_gpp_dt = sum(!is.na(GPP_DT_VUT_REF)),
    n_spring_gpp_nt = sum(!is.na(GPP_NT_VUT_REF)),
    frac_spring_dt = n_spring_gpp_dt / n_spring_days,
    frac_spring_nt = n_spring_gpp_nt / n_spring_days,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# mark all years, where <95% data coverage in spring
df_qc_spring <- df_qc_spring |> 
  mutate(
    pass_spring_dt = frac_spring_dt >= 0.95,
    pass_spring_nt = frac_spring_nt >= 0.95
  )

# join both data frames into one
df_qc <- df_qc_year |>
  left_join(df_qc_spring, by = c("site_id", "year")) |>
  mutate(
    pass_dt = pass_annual_dt & pass_spring_dt,
    pass_nt = pass_annual_nt & pass_spring_nt
  )
```

### Plot quality control variables
```{R}
# Visualize yearly data quality
ggplot(df_qc, aes(x = year, y = frac_gpp_dt)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~site_id) +
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Fraction valid yearly GPP days",
    title = "Yearly data coverage for GPP_DT_VUT_REF"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The quality control check shows that for all 5 sites the measurement of GPP_NT_VUT_REF and GPP_DT_VUT_REF contain no gaps. Therefore, the analysis of GPP can be conducted at all 5 sites over the entire period of recordings for each station. 

## Smooth daily GPP data
Extensive comparison of parameter combinations
```{R}
# load scripts
source("../R/ssa_functions.R")
source("../R/plot_ssa.R")

# calculate smoothed curve for all parameter combinations
df_ssa_compare <- compare_ssa_grid(
  data = df_all_selected_stations,
  sites = c("BE-Vie", "CH-Lae", "DE-Hai", "DE-Tha", "US-Ha1"),
  yr = 2017,
  var = GPP_DT_VUT_REF,
  doy_col = "doy",
  L_values = c(30, 45, 60, 90, 120),
  comp_max = 5
)

# plot result
dir.create("../figures/ssa_settings")

sites_to_plot <- c("BE-Vie", "CH-Lae", "DE-Tha", "DE-Hai", "US-Ha1")
year_to_plot <- 2017

for (s in sites_to_plot) {
  p <- plot_ssa_initial_parameters(df_ssa_compare, site = s)
  
  ggsave(
    filename = paste0("../figures/ssa_settings/ssa_settings_", s, "_", year_to_plot, ".png"),
    plot = p,
    width = 14,
    height = 10,
    dpi = 300
  )
}

```

As explained in Golyandina & Zhigljavsky (2013) Singular Spectrum Analysis two main parameters can be set L = window length and c = reconstructed components. Depending on how these two parameters are defined the smoothed GPP curve still contains more or less of the variability in the original data. To gain an overview how different settings for the two parameters affect the smoothing of daily GPP data for each of the 5 sites, 5 different window lengths and including components 1-5, were tested. The result showed that for all sites including 4 or 5 components already adds a lot of variability to the curve to just extract the annual cycle of daily GPP. Therefore, these components are no longer considered. The window lengths 30 and 45 were also discarded, because for certain sites, like DE-Hai, with such a small window length there was still a lot of variability visible in the curve. Across all sites the most consistent smoothed curves were produced by the following combination: L60C2, L90C2, L90C3, L120C3. In the next step these 4 combinations will be tested for multiple years to evaluate which one of the combinations produced the most consistent smoothed daily GPP curves. 

Comparison of select combinations of parameters across all sites and multiple years
```{R}
# load scripts
source("../R/ssa_functions.R")
source("../R/plot_ssa.R")

sites_to_plot <- c("BE-Vie", "CH-Lae", "DE-Hai", "DE-Tha", "US-Ha1")
years_to_plot <- c(2005, 2010, 2015, 2020)

dir.create("../figures/ssa_settings", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

for (yr in years_to_plot) {
  
  df_ssa_compare <- compare_ssa_settings(
    data = df_all_selected_stations,
    sites = sites_to_plot,
    yr = yr,
    var = GPP_DT_VUT_REF,
    doy_col = "doy"
  )
  
  for (s in sites_to_plot) {
    p <- plot_ssa_select_parameters(df_ssa_compare, site = s)
    
    ggsave(
      filename = paste0("../figures/ssa_settings/ssa_settings_", s, "_", yr, ".png"),
      plot = p,
      width = 10,
      height = 8,
      dpi = 300
    )
  }
}
```

The final SSA parameterization was chosen to preserve the dominant seasonal development of daily GPP while minimizing short-term variability that could bias threshold-based SOS detection. Across the inspected site-years, the combination window length `L = 90` and reconstruction of components `c = 1:2` provided the most consistent compromise between smoothness and retention of the seasonal GPP trajectory. Compared with L60_C2, it reduced residual short-term variability more effectively, while compared with L90_C3 it avoided reintroducing oscillations. Although L120_C3 also produced plausible seasonal curves, it tended to smooth the seasonal transitions more strongly. Therefore, L90_C2 was selected for the main analysis.

Therefore, these analysis will proceed with the identification of the SOS as proposed in Gu et al. (2022) using smoothed daily GPP values calculated with the Singular Spectrum Analysis with parameters set to the values described above.

Smooth daytime GPP measurements:
```{R}
source("../R/ssa_functions.R")
df_ssa <- run_ssa_all(
  data = df_all_selected_stations,
  L = 90,
  comps = 1:2,
)
```

## Identification of the SOS
As proposed in Gu et al. (2022) and in Chen et al. (2023) for the identification of the Start of Season this analysis will use the approach of identifying the SOS based on a threshold of multi year maximum daily smoothed GPP. Two threshold levels will be tested: 15% of multi year maximum daily smoothed GPP (Gu et al., 2022) and 10% of multi year maximum daily smoothed GPP (Chen et al., 2023). The 10% threshold will also be tested, because it was used in the context of late spring frost, whereas the 10% threshold was used in the context of drought impacts. 

Formula for SOS threshold:
$$
T_{\mathrm{s}} = a \times \max_{t,y}\left(GPP_{\mathrm{smooth},s,t,y}\right)
$$

Two threshold levels will be tested a = {0.10,0.15}. 

Each site has one threshold that is defined as either 10% or 15% of the averaged maximum smoothed daily GPP over all the years. 

Note: For each site, the first and last year of the time series were excluded from SOS analyses based on SSA-smoothed daily GPP in order to reduce edge effects/artefacts associated with smoothing at the boundaries of the series.
```{R}
# remove first and last year
df_ssa_core <- df_ssa |>
  group_by(site_id) |>
  filter(
    year > min(year),
    year < max(year)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# load functions
source("../R/GPP_SOS.R")

# calculate 15 % threshold for each site
thresholds_site_t15 <- calc_site_thresholds(
  data = df_ssa_core,
  gpp_col = "GPP_DT_smoothed",
  frac = 0.15
)

# calculate 10% threshold for each site
thresholds_site_t10 <- calc_site_thresholds(
  data = df_ssa_core,
  gpp_col = "GPP_DT_smoothed",
  frac = 0.10
)

# SOS for each site with 15% threshold
df_sos_t15 <- identify_sos(
  data = df_ssa_core,
  thresholds = thresholds_site_t15,
  gpp_col = "GPP_DT_smoothed",
  doy_col = "doy"
)

# SOS for each site with 10% threshold
df_sos_t10 <- identify_sos(
  data = df_ssa_core,
  thresholds = thresholds_site_t10,
  gpp_col = "GPP_DT_smoothed",
  doy_col = "doy"
)

```

Boxplot SOS for all sites
```{R}
ggplot(df_sos_t10, aes(x = site_id, y = SOS))+
  geom_boxplot()+
  ggtitle("Distribution of SOS for each site with 10% threshold")

ggplot(df_sos_t15, aes(x = site_id, y = SOS))+
  geom_boxplot()+
  ggtitle("Distribution of SOS for each site with 15% threshold")
```

For both thresholds there are significant differences between sites regarding the DOY of the SOS with BE-Vie having the lowest average with 57 and US-Ha1 the highest with an average of 121. But not only the average SOS varies across sites also the variability of SOS within a site across years varies. As expected the SOS shifts to a later DOY with 15% threshold compared to 10% threshold. However, the shift is not equally strong across sites. some sites show a more significant than others.

The differences in SOS between sites match with the annual plots of smoothed daily GPP. In the plots one can clearly see that the rise starts a lot earlier in BE-Vie compared to US-Ha1, where the curve often stays quite flat at the start of the year. So increase in daily GPP picks up later which is consistent with reaching a given GPP threshold later in the year.

Plot trend of SOS for each site
```{R}
ggplot(df_sos_t10, aes(x = year, y = SOS)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#0072B2", linewidth = 0.9) +
  stat_poly_eq(
    formula = y ~ x,
    aes(label = paste(..rr.label.., ..p.value.label.., sep = "*\", \"*")),
    parse = TRUE,
    label.x = "left",
    label.y = "top",
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ site_id) +
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Start of season (DOY)",
    title = "Interannual variation in SOS (10% threshold)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

ggplot(df_sos_t15, aes(x = year, y = SOS)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#0072B2", linewidth = 0.9) +
  stat_poly_eq(
    formula = y ~ x,
    aes(label = paste(..rr.label.., ..p.value.label.., sep = "*\", \"*")),
    parse = TRUE,
    label.x = "left",
    label.y = "top",
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ site_id) +
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Start of season (DOY)",
    title = "Interannual variation in SOS (15% threshold)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

When looking at the trend in SOS over the measurement period SOS has generally advanced over this period for multiple sites. For the 10% threshold this trend is statistically significant (p<0.05) for CH-Lae and US-Ha1, with CH-Lae showing a very clear advancement. For the 15% threshold only CH-Lae is statistically significant, again with a trend of very clear advancement.

## Identification of last spring forst after SOS
Calculate LSF after SOS with 10% and 15% threshold
```{R}
source("../R/GPP_LSF.R")

#calculate LSF after SOS with 10% threshold
lsf_results_10 <- calculate_lsf_after_sos(
  df = df_ssa_core,
  df_sos = df_sos_t10,
  frost_threshold = -2,
  temp_col = "TMIN_F_MDS"
)
df_lsf_daily_t10 <- lsf_results_10$daily
df_lsf_summary_t10 <- lsf_results_10$yearly_summary

# calculate LSF after SOS with 15% threshold
lsf_results_15 <- calculate_lsf_after_sos(
  df = df_ssa_core, # use data frame where the first and last year have been removed
  df_sos = df_sos_t15,
  frost_threshold = -2,
  temp_col = "TMIN_F_MDS"
)

df_lsf_daily_t15 <- lsf_results_15$daily
df_lsf_summary_t15 <- lsf_results_15$yearly_summary
```

Heatmap to visualize occurance of frost after SOS
```{R}
# combine threshold data frames
df_lsf_thr_combined <- bind_rows(
  df_lsf_summary_t10 |> mutate(threshold_label = "10%"),
  df_lsf_summary_t15 |> mutate(threshold_label = "15%"))

# df for heat map and plot
df_lsf_heat <- df_lsf_thr_combined |>
  mutate(
    has_frost_after_sos = !is.na(n_frost_days_after_SOS_until_LSF),
    event = ifelse(has_frost_after_sos, "Yes", "No"),
    threshold_label = factor(threshold_label, levels = c("10%", "15%"))
  )

ggplot(df_lsf_heat, aes(x = year, y = site_id, fill = event)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  facet_grid(threshold_label ~ .) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Yes" = "#D55E00", "No" = "#F0F0F0")) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = sort(unique(df_lsf_heat$year))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Occurrence of frost after SOS across sites and years",
    subtitle = "10% and 15% stand for the two different SOS-thresholds",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Site",
    fill = "Frost after SOS"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1)
  )
```

Using 15% of maximum smoothed daily GPP as the threshold for SOS results in many years without frost events after SOS, as shown in the heatmap above. This is noteworthy because the 15% threshold proposed by Gu et al. (2022) was used in the context of analysing drought effects rather than late spring frost exposure. By contrast, Chen et al. (2023), who also considered late spring frost, used a lower threshold of 10% of maximum smoothed daily GPP. Repeating the analysis with this lower threshold increased the number of years identified with frost after SOS across all sites. This indicates that the estimated occurrence of post-SOS frost is sensitive to the chosen GPP threshold, with the 10% threshold capturing more years in which frost occurs after the onset of seasonal photosynthetic activity.

However, even when applying the 10% threshold, many years still show no frost events after SOS. Strong site-specific differences are evident. The two sites with the fewest post-SOS frost years are DE-Hai and US-Ha1, and the effect of threshold choice is particularly pronounced at these sites, where post-SOS frost is generally rare. In contrast, other sites, especially BE-Vie and DE-Tha, show frost after SOS in a much larger proportion of years.

An additional important result is that all European sites show a frost event after SOS in 2017. This suggests that the approach consistently captures the well-documented late spring frost event of that year across sites.

To gain an understanding how many days lay between the SOS and the last frost event the distribution of doy_last_frost - SOS will be calculate for all years with a frost event after SOS
```{R}
# filter out years with frost after SOS
df_lsf_events <- df_lsf_thr_combined |>
  mutate(has_frost_after_sos = !is.na(n_frost_days_after_SOS_until_LSF))|>
  filter(has_frost_after_sos) |>
  mutate(
    days_between_sos_and_lsf = last_frost_doy - SOS,
    threshold_label = factor(threshold_label, levels = c("10%", "15%"))
  )

y_max <- ceiling(max(df_lsf_events$days_between_sos_and_lsf, na.rm = TRUE) / 10) * 10

ggplot(df_lsf_events, aes(x = site_id, y = days_between_sos_and_lsf, fill = threshold_label)) +
  geom_boxplot(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, y_max),
    breaks = seq(0, y_max, by = 10)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Time between SOS and last spring frost",
    subtitle = "Only years with at least one frost event after SOS",
    x = "Site",
    y = "Days between SOS and last frost",
    fill = "Threshold"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

Overview on covered years and number of years with events for each site
```{R}
df_lsf_event_counts <- df_lsf_thr_combined|>
  mutate(has_frost_after_sos = !is.na(n_frost_days_after_SOS_until_LSF))|>
  group_by(site_id, threshold_label) |>
  summarise(
    n_total_years = n(),
    n_event_years = sum(has_frost_after_sos, na.rm = TRUE),
    percentage_event_years = round((n_event_years/n_total_years)*100,2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(site_id, threshold_label)

df_lsf_event_counts
```

The boxplots show that sites with frequent post-SOS frost events also tend to exhibit a larger interannual variation in the number of days between SOS and the last spring frost. This is especially the case for BE-Vie and DE-Tha, where the interval between SOS and last frost can be several weeks and varies strongly between years. CH-Lae shows smaller but still variable intervals, while DE-Hai and US-Ha1 mostly exhibit short intervals when such events occur. Interpretation of the boxplots for DE-Hai and US-Ha1 should be treated with caution because they are based on very few event years. For these sites, the boxplots are mainly descriptive and do not allow robust conclusions about the full distribution of post-SOS frost timing.

In general, the 10% threshold results in larger values of days between SOS and last spring frost than the 15% threshold. This is expected because the lower threshold defines SOS earlier in the season. Consequently, it expands the time window during which frost events can occur after SOS.

## Analysis of GPP after last frost across European sites
Filter all years with last frost event after SOS for all European sites
```{R}
eu_sites <- c("BE-Vie", "CH-Lae", "DE-Hai", "DE-Tha")

# yearly event information based on the 10% threshold
df_events <- df_lsf_summary_t10 |>
  filter(site_id %in% eu_sites) |>
  filter(!is.na(last_frost_after_sos_doy)) |>
  select(site_id, year, last_frost_after_sos_doy)

# join event years with GPP data
df_event_base <- df_ssa_core |>
  filter(site_id %in% eu_sites) |>
  inner_join(df_events, by = c("site_id", "year"))

# all years as reference of GPP
df_ref_base <- df_ssa_core |>
  filter(site_id %in% eu_sites)
```

Combine daily GPP data with event years
```{R}
# load functions to calculate GPP after LSF
source("../R/GPP_after_LSF.R")

# reference windows for GPP after last frost
windows_tbl <- tibble(
  window_label = c("1-15", "16-30", "31-60", "61-90", "91-180"),
  start_day = c(1, 16, 31, 61, 91),
  end_day   = c(15, 30, 60, 90, 180)
)

# create data frame for GPP after LSF in event year
df_event_gpp <- pmap_dfr(
  list(windows_tbl$window_label, windows_tbl$start_day, windows_tbl$end_day),
  function(window_label, start_day, end_day) {
    calc_event_window_gpp(
      data = df_event_base,
      start_day = start_day,
      end_day = end_day,
      window_label = window_label
    )
  }
)

# create data frame for GPP in same timeperiod as after LSF over all years
df_reference_all <- pmap_dfr(
  list(windows_tbl$window_label, windows_tbl$start_day, windows_tbl$end_day),
  function(window_label, start_day, end_day) {
    calc_reference_window_gpp(
      event_data = df_event_base,
      ref_data = df_ref_base,
      start_day = start_day,
      end_day = end_day,
      window_label = window_label
    )
  }
)

# create data frame for GPP in same timeperiod as after LSF over 5 years before event
df_reference_prev5 <- pmap_dfr(
  list(windows_tbl$window_label, windows_tbl$start_day, windows_tbl$end_day),
  function(window_label, start_day, end_day) {
    calc_reference_window_gpp_prev5(
      event_data = df_event_base,
      ref_data = df_ref_base,
      start_day = start_day,
      end_day = end_day,
      window_label = window_label
    )
  }
)

# data frame with anomalies in GPP after LSF over all years
df_gpp_compare <- df_event_gpp |>
  rename(event_year = year) |>
  left_join(
    df_reference_all,
    by = c("site_id", "event_year", "post_lsf_window")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    anomaly_gpp_dt = mean_gpp_dt - ref_gpp_dt,
    anomaly_gpp_smoothed = mean_gpp_smoothed - ref_gpp_smoothed,
    rel_anomaly_gpp_dt = 100 * (mean_gpp_dt - ref_gpp_dt) / ref_gpp_dt,
    rel_anomaly_gpp_smoothed = 100 * (mean_gpp_smoothed - ref_gpp_smoothed) / ref_gpp_smoothed
  )

# data frame with anomalies in GPP after LSF compared to 5 years before event
df_gpp_compare_prev5 <- df_event_gpp |>
  rename(event_year = year) |>
  left_join(
    df_reference_prev5,
    by = c("site_id", "event_year", "post_lsf_window")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    anomaly_gpp_dt_prev5 = mean_gpp_dt - ref5_gpp_dt,
    anomaly_gpp_smoothed_prev5 = mean_gpp_smoothed - ref5_gpp_smoothed,
    rel_anomaly_gpp_dt_prev5 = 100 * (mean_gpp_dt - ref5_gpp_dt) / ref5_gpp_dt,
    rel_anomaly_gpp_smoothed_prev5 = 100 * (mean_gpp_smoothed - ref5_gpp_smoothed) / ref5_gpp_smoothed
  )
# only keep the events that had 5 years before the event because we work with 5 year mean
df_gpp_compare_prev5_valid <- df_gpp_compare_prev5 |>
  filter(n_ref_years == 5)

```

Plot the relative anomaly in GPP after LSF for all events
```{R}
ggplot(df_gpp_compare, aes(x = post_lsf_window, y = rel_anomaly_gpp_smoothed, group = event_year)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~site_id) +
  labs(
    title = "Relative anomaly of smoothed daily GPP after last spring frost",
    x = "Days after last spring frost",
    y = "Relative GPP anomaly (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

## Analyze GPP development after LSF for 2017 event
*Analysis of anomalies compared to average of all site years:*

Plot relative anomaly of GPP
```{R}
df_gpp_compare_2017 <- df_gpp_compare |>
  filter(event_year == 2017) |>
  mutate(
    post_lsf_window = factor(
      post_lsf_window,
      levels = c("1-15", "16-30", "31-60", "61-90", "91-180")
    )
  )
ggplot(df_gpp_compare_2017, aes(x = post_lsf_window, y = rel_anomaly_gpp_smoothed, group = site_id)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~site_id) +
  labs(
    title = "Relative anomaly of smoothed daily GPP after last spring frost in 2017",
    x = "Days after last spring frost",
    y = "Relative GPP anomaly (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

Plot the course of mean smoothed daily GPP after LSF 2017 compared to mean smoothed daily GPP over all years
```{R}
df_gpp_compare_2017_long <- df_gpp_compare_2017 |>
  select(site_id, post_lsf_window, mean_gpp_smoothed, ref_gpp_smoothed) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(mean_gpp_smoothed, ref_gpp_smoothed),
    names_to = "series",
    values_to = "gpp"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    series = recode(
      series,
      mean_gpp_smoothed = "Event year 2017",
      ref_gpp_smoothed = "Mean of all other years"
    )
  )

ggplot(df_gpp_compare_2017_long, aes(x = post_lsf_window, y = gpp, group = series, color = series)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~site_id) +
  labs(
    title = "Smoothed GPP after last spring frost: 2017 vs. long-term mean",
    x = "Days after last spring frost",
    y = "Mean smoothed daily GPP",
    color = ""
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

Boxplot smoothed daily GPP
```{R}
ggplot(df_gpp_compare_2017_long, aes(x = post_lsf_window, y = gpp, fill = series)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~site_id) +
  labs(
    title = "Smoothed GPP after last spring frost: 2017 vs. long-term mean",
    x = "Days after last spring frost",
    y = "Mean smoothed daily GPP",
    fill = ""
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

*Analysis of anomalies compared to 5 year average previous to event:*

Plot relative anomaly of GPP
```{R}
df_gpp_compare_prev5_2017 <- df_gpp_compare_prev5 |>
  filter(event_year == 2017) |>
  mutate(
    post_lsf_window = factor(
      post_lsf_window,
      levels = c("1-15", "16-30", "31-60", "61-90", "91-180")
    )
  )

  ggplot(df_gpp_compare_prev5_2017, aes(x = post_lsf_window, y = rel_anomaly_gpp_smoothed_prev5, group = site_id)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~site_id) +
  labs(
    title = "Relative anomaly of smoothed daily GPP in 2017 vs. mean of the previous 5 years",
    x = "Days after last spring frost",
    y = "Relative GPP anomaly (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

Plot the course of mean smoothed daily GPP after LSF 2017 compared to mean smoothed daily GPP over previous 5 years
```{R}
df_gpp_compare_prev5_2017_long <- df_gpp_compare_prev5_2017 |>
  select(site_id, post_lsf_window, mean_gpp_smoothed, ref5_gpp_smoothed) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(mean_gpp_smoothed, ref5_gpp_smoothed),
    names_to = "series",
    values_to = "gpp"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    series = recode(
      series,
      mean_gpp_smoothed = "Event year 2017",
      ref5_gpp_smoothed = "Mean of previous 5 years"
    )
  )

ggplot(df_gpp_compare_prev5_2017_long, aes(x = post_lsf_window, y = gpp, group = series, color = series)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~site_id) +
  labs(
    title = "Smoothed GPP after last spring frost: 2017 vs. 5-year-mean",
    x = "Days after last spring frost",
    y = "Mean smoothed daily GPP",
    color = ""
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

Boxplot smoothed daily GPP
```{R}
ggplot(df_gpp_compare_prev5_2017_long, aes(x = post_lsf_window, y = gpp, fill = series)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~site_id) +
  labs(
    title = "Smoothed GPP after last spring frost: 2017 vs. 5 year mean",
    x = "Days after last spring frost",
    y = "Mean smoothed daily GPP",
    fill = ""
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

*Interpretation of result on the analysis of 2017 event*

**Introduction to analysis**

To gain an understanding of the effect of LSF after SOS the known event in 2017 was analysed for all 4 European sites. For this the development of smoothed daily GPP after the event was first compared to the mean smoothed daily GPP over all the site years for the period in the following 180 days after the event. In a second step this comparison was repeated but with the mean smoothed daily GPP of the 5 years previous to the event. The analysis with the 5 years previous to the event was included, because the mean over all site years could potentially be influenced by long term trends in GPP. 

**Analysis of GPP post LSF compared to average over all site years**



## Literature
Gu, H., Qiao, Y., Xi, Z., Rossi, S., Smith, N. G., Liu, J., & Chen, L. (2022). Warming-induced increase in carbon uptake is linked to earlier spring phenology in temperate and boreal forests. Nature Communications, 13(1), 3698. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-31496-w

Golyandina, N., & Zhigljavsky, A. (2013). Singular Spectrum Analysis for Time Series. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-642-34913-3

Chen, L., Keski-Saari, S., Kontunen-Soppela, S., Zhu, X., Zhou, X., Hänninen, H., Pumpanen, J., Mola-Yudego, B., Wu, D., & Berninger, F. (2023). Immediate and carry-over effects of late-spring frost and growing season drought on forest gross primary productivity capacity in the Northern Hemisphere. Global Change Biology, 29(14), 3924–3940. https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.16751
